schools.summary: The arrival at the wilderness of Sin — 'on the fifteenth day of the second month' (Sin reached exactly one month after the departure from Egypt on the fifteenth of the first month, Ellicott); the month's provision carried out of Egypt now all spent (Henry, Wesley), the need exactly accounting for the murmuring and the miracle; the journey from Elim (twelve wells, seventy palms) into the barren tract of Sin where no food was to be had; God letting their own store run out before supplying his own, that they learn dependence; the barren wilderness the very place God chose to spread a table.

themes: the fifteenth day of the second month
the stage set for the manna

schools.summary: The third murmuring — 'the whole congregation... murmured' (not some only but all gathered into mobs as in a conspiracy, murmuring with one consent, though the universal term may admit exception, Calvin); the third murmuring since the Exodus — the first at the Red Sea, the second at Marah (Ex 15:24), now for want of bread, a settled habit of discontent forming (Ellicott); 'against Moses and Aaron,' God's viceregents, so to murmur against them was to murmur against God (Wesley); betraying the degraded carnal state of minds beaten down by slavery, quick to forget the wonders wrought (Clarke); real need no excuse for the unbelief that murmurs instead of praying.

The extravagance of their complaint — 'would to God we had died... in the land of Egypt' (the Hebrew containing no name of God, simply 'would that we had died,' perhaps an allusion to the plagues, as if wishing to have perished with the Egyptians, Ellicott); 'when we sat by the flesh pots' (the great boilers where taskmasters cooked food for the slave-gangs, the people remembering the fare but forgetting the whips, painting bondage as plenty, Clarke); the cruel charge that Moses brought them out to starve them, a slander on their deliverers and on God; so undervaluing their deliverance as to wish themselves dead in bondage (Wesley); unbelief blotting out past mercies and future promise.

themes: would to God we had died
to kill this assembly with hunger

schools.summary: The promise of bread from heaven — 'I will rain bread from heaven for you' (no natural product of the desert, else they had had it before, Clarke; a celestial, divine supply, not mere food out of the air, Ellicott); 'gather a certain rate every day' (a daily portion gathered fresh each morning, in continual dependence on God); 'that I may prove them, whether they will walk in my law' (the daily gathering a trial of obedience and trust); God's kindness feeding so ungrateful a people, yet joining a test to reveal their hearts (Calvin); the typology — Israel a figure of the church of the firstborn receiving bread from heaven, the manna prefiguring Christ the true Bread (John 6:31) and teaching the prayer for daily bread (Mt 6:11, Wesley).

themes: I will rain bread from heaven
gather a certain rate every day

schools.summary: The double portion for the sixth day — 'on the sixth day... twice as much as they gather daily' (a double portion given against the Sabbath, on which none would fall, reckoned the sixth of the week from the first giving, Ellicott/Gill); the purpose — since the Sabbath is brought in, the law of holy rest already in view, God making trial whether they would honour the day, gathering double beforehand and resting (Calvin); the standing weekly miracle — the bread falling six days and not the seventh, the sixth day's double portion keeping while on other days the surplus bred worms; the rest so honoured by God that he would not send manna upon it; the Sabbath treated as already known.

The promise that they shall know the LORD — 'at even, then ye shall know that the LORD hath brought you out' (by evening, when the quails would come, proof that it was the LORD, not merely Moses and Aaron, who led them); the rebuke implied — after all the miracles they still spoke as though deliverance were the work of Moses and Aaron who brought them into trouble, God now showing himself their deliverer and provider by feeding them (Clarke); directly answering the wicked taunt of v3 that they had been betrayed into the wilderness, God vindicating his servants by owning the deliverance as his own (Calvin); 'at even' pointing to the quails that evening (Ellicott); God answering slander with food.

themes: the LORD hath brought you out

schools.summary: The glory of the LORD and the murmuring heard — 'in the morning ye shall see the glory of the LORD' (chiefly the manna lying round the host at dawn, God's glory in raining bread about their tents, distinct from the appearing in the cloud at v10, Ellicott/Gill); 'he heareth your murmurings against the LORD' (their grumbling, though spoken against Moses and Aaron, in truth against the LORD, and all heard in heaven); 'what are we, that ye murmur against us?' (the leaders owning they were but instruments, to murmur against them being to murmur against God); the phrase 'the glory of the Lord' seeming almost distinct from the Lord, since said to hear, though it may stand for God in his manifested presence (Clarke).

themes: ye shall see the glory of the LORD

schools.summary: The double gift and the true object of the murmuring — 'in the evening flesh to eat, and in the morning bread to the full' (the evening flesh the quails, the morning bread the manna, given in such plenty as to eat to the full, meeting both the craving for meat and the need of bread, Clarke); the repeated insistence that it is the LORD who gives, not Moses, the leaders but the channel; 'what are we?' (only his servants obeying his commands, Moses and Aaron refusing to stand as the object of thanks or blame); 'your murmurings are not against us, but against the LORD' (the discontent striking past the visible leaders to God who appointed their way); grace charging home the sin yet still promising the food.

themes: in the evening flesh, in the morning bread
not against us, but against the LORD

schools.summary: The summons before the LORD — 'Come near before the LORD: for he hath heard your murmurings' (the people bidden through Aaron to present themselves before God's manifested presence); the place — some appointed spot where the Lord made known his presence, perhaps an early tent of meeting before the great tabernacle, above all the pillar of cloud, the visible sign of God among them (Clarke); the summons a kind of arraignment, Moses citing the murmurers as criminals before God's tribunal, that they learn their grumbling was heard and judged (Calvin); a murmuring people called to stand before the glory they had provoked, to receive both rebuke and the proof of God's care.

themes: the cloud the sign of God

schools.summary: The appearing of the glory — 'as Aaron spake... they looked toward the wilderness, and the glory of the LORD appeared in the cloud' (even while the word was delivered, before he finished, the glory broke forth, God sealing his servant's message); the people turning toward the open desert where the cloud stood; the cloud the pillar that led them, the divine glory shining forth, an extraordinary and sudden brightness bursting from it (Ellicott, Wesley); Aaron acting as spokesman and minister of Moses to the people as earlier to Pharaoh (Ex 7:1, Clarke); the appearing both to awe the murmurers and to authenticate the promise of food; God answering being murmured against by drawing near.

themes: the glory of the LORD appeared in the cloud

schools.summary: The LORD speaking from the cloud — the LORD spake 'out of the bright and glorious cloud' that had just appeared, the manifested presence uttering its message (Gill); the verse showing that the great promise of flesh and bread, already announced by Moses and Aaron, rested not on their own word but on God's express command; Moses shown to have done nothing of himself but faithfully and modestly discharged the office of a minister, and, unless he had spoken according to God's word, he would have been rash in promising what was not his to give (Calvin); the divine word ratifying the human announcement; God's servants may speak boldly what God has commanded.

The promise confirmed and its end — 'I have heard the murmurings of the children of Israel' (God confirming what Moses and Aaron said, owning he had noticed their grumbling and was displeased, yet would answer it with food, Gill); 'at even ye shall eat flesh, and in the morning... filled with bread' (the quails by evening, the manna by morning, to the full).

Wesley: 'ye shall know that I am the LORD your God' proved both his power as 'the LORD' and his favour as 'your God' — when God plagued Egypt it was that they might know he is the LORD, but when he fed Israel it was that they might know he is their God; God answering murmuring not with plagues but with the bread of heaven, every meal preaching the covenant God.

themes: at even flesh, in the morning bread
that ye may know I am the LORD your God

schools.summary: The quails and the dew — 'at even the quails came up, and covered the camp' (great flights of migratory quail that pass in immense masses over Egypt and Arabia, the 'feathered fowl' of Ps 78:27, not locusts, Henry/Ellicott); so tame or wearied with flight that the people might take as many as they pleased, the meat given without labour (Wesley); the name selav from a root 'to be quiet,' the quail living securely among the corn (Clarke); 'in the morning the dew lay round about the host' (the dew falling first, with which the manna came); flesh in the evening, bread in the morning, exactly as promised; the quails here mercy, later given in wrath when they lusted (Num 11:31).

themes: the quails came up and covered the camp
the dew lay round about the host

schools.summary: The appearance of the manna — 'when the dew that lay was gone up... a small round thing, as small as the hoar frost' (the morning dew drawn up by the sun leaving the manna, the dew falling first and the manna upon it, Clarke; fine round grains scattered like frost over the wilderness); the long dispute over what the manna was — two natural substances of the region compared, but neither answering to the manna of Scripture, which fell in such quantity, kept one day and not the next, doubled on the sixth and failed on the seventh, the miracle not to be explained away (Ellicott); no natural product but miraculously created, though like dew condensed into small round grains (Calvin); the bread of heaven in a lowly, frost-like form.

themes: the dew that lay was gone up
as small as the hoar frost

schools.summary: The naming of the manna — Moses' answer 'This is the bread which the LORD hath given you to eat' (the unknown thing explained as God's gift, the promised bread from heaven); the typology — this bread a figure of Christ, the true Bread from heaven (John 6:31); God's best gifts at first a mystery to those who receive them, the gift so above nature it had no name.

debates.question: Does the Hebrew 'man hu' mean 'It is manna' or 'What is this?'

sides.position: 'What is this?' (or 'This is a gift') — the AV's 'It is manna' is a mistranslation that contradicts itself, for if they named it they knew it; the food took its name from their wondering question
the root may mean 'a prepared thing' or 'an assigned portion,' the name expressing a gift given

themes: they wist not what it was
the bread which the LORD hath given

schools.summary: The rule of gathering — 'gather of it every man according to his eating, an omer for every man... according to the number of your persons' (a set measure, an omer about three English pints, for every person in the tent, reckoned by the souls to be fed, Ellicott); each to gather according to his immediate need, none to lay up a store, the supply for the day not for hoarding; the omer a measure named from a root 'to press or collect' (Clarke); the daily measure prescribed, the Sabbath exception coming later (Calvin); the equity — a fixed portion a head, neither more for the greedy nor less for the weak, the gathering matching the eating; God measuring his provision to need, forbidding covetous laying-up that distrusts tomorrow.

themes: according to the number of your persons

schools.summary: The gathering, more and less — 'the children of Israel did so, and gathered, some more, some less' (going out in the morning when the manna lay, gathering as directed); the difference by the size of households, each gathering in proportion to the persons he had to feed, the able for the infirm (Clarke, Keil); the obedience not perfect or greatly to be praised, since some soon gathered more than allowed and broke the rule, the 'more and less' within God's appointment but human greed presently exceeding it (Calvin); the going-out a figure of believers leaving the world for Christ to gather the bread of life (Gill); the gift and the duty joined — the bread given freely yet they must go out and gather it.

themes: the children of Israel did so
the gift and the duty joined

schools.summary: The equal sufficiency of the manna — 'he that gathered much had nothing over, and he that gathered little had no lack' (measured by the omer, each household found to have just an omer per person, neither surplus nor shortfall); the providential proportion — each had gathered according to the number he had to feed, some over-estimating, some under, yet on measuring each had exactly his omer apiece (Clarke, Ellicott); the hand of God so ordering the gathering that none lacked and none abounded; the apostle's lesson of equality, the abundance of some supplying the want of others (2 Cor 8:15); God's provision exactly fitting his people's need, enough for all, excess for none.

The rule of daily dependence — 'Let no man leave of it till the morning' (each day's portion eaten that day, nothing hoarded, tomorrow's supply sought afresh from God); divinely instructed, that Israel realise their absolute dependence on God day by day (Ellicott), God supplying their need anew each morning that they continually look to him (Calvin); the connection — God would have them take no thought for the morrow, the Lord's prayer's 'Give us this day our daily bread' (Mt 6:11) perhaps founded on this very circumstance (Clarke); learning to sleep quietly though not a bit of bread were in the camp, trusting God for the morrow (Wesley); weaning them from the anxious storing-up that springs from distrust.

themes: let no man leave of it till the morning
give us this day our daily bread

schools.summary: The disobedience and corruption of the hoarded manna — 'some of them left of it until the morning, and it bred worms, and stank' (not all but some, in distrust or covetousness, keeping back a portion against the command, the hoarded manna rotting with worms, Gill); no natural decay, for the same manna kept over the Sabbath by God's command bred no worms (Ex 16:24), the corruption here a judgment on the disobedience, Ellicott; God rendering the sin irksome and unprofitable, the covetous laying-up bringing nothing but stench and worms, making disobedience its own punishment (Clarke); 'Moses was wroth' (righteous displeasure at their unbelief); what is hoarded in distrust of God corrupts.

The daily gathering and melting of the manna — 'they gathered it every morning, every man according to his eating' (gathered in the early hours, till about the fourth hour, ten o'clock, by the old reckoning, taken fresh at the day's beginning before the sun grew strong, Gill); the wonder that bread should be provided anew every single morning for a whole nation, falling with the dew; 'when the sun waxed hot, it melted' (what was not gathered in the cool dissolving under the day's heat, so the gathering could not be put off); the discipline — taught to rise early and seek their bread while it could be had, the manna rewarding diligence and punishing sloth; the bread of heaven, like Christ and his grace, to be sought early and in season.

themes: when the sun waxed hot, it melted

schools.summary: The double portion and the rulers' surprise — 'on the sixth day they gathered twice as much, two omers for one man' (not gathered more by effort but twice the manna lay on the ground, a double supply against the Sabbath on which none would fall); a convincing miracle, the manna falling six days and never the seventh, double on the sixth, no natural product but ordered by God in honour of his Sabbath (Clarke); 'all the rulers... came and told Moses' (surprised at the unwonted abundance, coming for an explanation, Ellicott); the dense ignorance even of the rulers, who had heard Moses foretell it (v5) yet were taken aback (Calvin); God providing exactly as he had said, giving occasion to teach the Sabbath.

themes: on the sixth day twice as much
the rulers came and told Moses

schools.summary: The Sabbath rest explained — 'to morrow is the rest of the holy sabbath unto the LORD' (the sixth day's double gift given because the seventh was holy rest, on which they were not to gather; bake and seethe today, lay up the remainder which alone would keep); the practical provision — the labour of preparing food done on the sixth day that the seventh be wholly rest, God so arranging his gift as to make Sabbath-keeping possible; the day called 'holy' and 'unto the LORD,' set apart for him.

debates.question: Was the Sabbath now first instituted, or already known before Sinai?

sides.position: already known and kept before Sinai, indeed from creation (Gen 2:3) — Moses speaks of 'the holy sabbath' as familiar and established, nothing intimating it was now first given; the manna honoured a Sabbath already in being
some have supposed the Sabbath was here first given or re-instituted and made binding on Israel

themes: to morrow is the rest of the holy sabbath
bake today, lay up the rest

schools.summary: The Sabbath manna preserved — 'they laid it up till the morning, as Moses bade: and it did not stink, neither was there any worm therein' (the remainder of the sixth day's double portion, what they had not baked or boiled, kept overnight at Moses' word, in trust of his promise, Gill); the wonder that the very manna which on any other day bred worms and stank (v20) now on the Sabbath remained sound and sweet; the contrast proving the weekday corruption no natural process but a judgment, and this preservation no natural keeping but a miracle, God making the same substance corrupt or keep according to his command, that the Sabbath be honoured; the reward of obedient trust.

themes: they laid it up till the morning

schools.summary: The Sabbath eating and the manna's absence — 'eat that to day; for to day is a sabbath unto the LORD' (the portion kept from the sixth day to be their food on the seventh, baked, boiled, or as it was, having no other, none falling that day, Gill); 'to day ye shall not find it in the field' (the manna, lying every other morning, not appearing on the Sabbath, the people needing not and unable to gather, the day one of rest); the Sabbath set forth with its essential character of a weekly rest, marked by its name, 'a rest unto the LORD' (Ellicott); God supplying the rest-day beforehand and proving its holiness by withholding the manna, the empty field a sermon on the Sabbath.

themes: to day is a sabbath unto the LORD
ye shall not find it in the field

schools.summary: The weekly rule of the manna — 'six days ye shall gather it' (day after day, every morning, through all their long sojourn in the wilderness, going out to gather their portion, Gill); 'on the seventh day, which is the sabbath, in it there shall be none' (no manna falling on the Sabbath, nothing to gather, the rest-day marked by the very absence of the bread); no passing provision but a settled order, the manna falling six days and never the seventh for the whole forty years, a continual testimony to the Sabbath; six days of labour and gathering, one of rest, mirroring creation and the commandment soon given at Sinai, the week of work and rest impressed on Israel before the law.

themes: six days ye shall gather it
on the seventh day there shall be none
work and rest before the law

schools.summary: The unbelief of the Sabbath gatherers — 'there went out some of the people on the seventh day for to gather, and they found none' (not all but some, in defiance or distrust of Moses' word, going out to seek manna on the day they were told none would fall); the second transgression in the matter of the manna (the first the hoarding of v20), trespassing upon the holy rest and dishonouring the Sabbath (Calvin); the root a 'monstrous greediness' springing from unbelief, not crediting what Moses plainly declared, refusing to take God at his word; the bare ground rebuking their unbelief, God making good his word in withholding as in giving; distrust leading to needless labour and broken commands.

themes: there went out some on the seventh day
unbelief rebuked by the empty ground

schools.summary: God's rebuke of persistent disobedience — 'How long refuse ye to keep my commandments and my laws?' (a reproof through Moses to the whole people, prompted by the Sabbath gatherers, the Lord who had seen them go out displeased and speaking out of the cloud, Gill); the force of 'how long' — not a first or isolated fault but one of a string, the people having already broken one precept by keeping the manna overnight (v20) and now another in spirit by gathering on the Sabbath (Ellicott); the weight God lays on these seemingly small commands, the keeping or breaking of his rule about the manna and the rest-day being the keeping or refusing of 'my commandments and my laws'; the 'how long' of a patience tried; God reproving rather than destroying.

themes: to keep my commandments and my laws

schools.summary: The Sabbath a gift, and the charge to abide — 'the LORD hath given you the sabbath' (the rest-day named not as a burden but a gift bestowed); 'therefore he giveth you on the sixth day the bread of two days' (the double portion a token of the gift, God providing beforehand that they rest without want); 'abide ye every man in his place... let no man go out on the seventh day' (staying at home, not roaming out for manna or common business, the rest filled with religious exercises, Clarke); some Jews taking 'abide in his place' so strictly as to keep the posture they woke in, but this slavish letter rightly rejected — the command forbids the labour of going out, not all movement (Ellicott); the Sabbath presented as a kindness.

themes: the LORD hath given you the sabbath
abide ye every man in his place
the day a gift, not a burden

schools.summary: The people's rest — 'so the people rested on the seventh day' (they did not go out of their tents in quest of manna as on working days, but kept the day as one of rest from labour, Gill); the turn from the foregoing disobedience — where some had hoarded and others gone out to gather, now the people as a whole submit and rest; not merely a single Sabbath but the beginning of a continued observance in succeeding generations, the wilderness teaching of the manna fixing the Sabbath in Israel's life before the law thundered it from Sinai; the fruit of God's patient dealing, bringing a murmuring people to honour his rest.

The name, look, and taste of the manna — 'the house of Israel called the name thereof Manna' (until now no settled name, the word arisen from their first question 'What is this?' v15; 'manna' a Greek shaping of the Hebrew 'man,' first used by the LXX, Ellicott); 'like coriander seed, white' (small round white grains, as before likened to hoar frost); 'the taste of it was like wafers made with honey' (sweet and pleasant, thin cakes sweetened with honey, elsewhere likened to fresh oil, Num 11:8); Moses dwelling on the description and repeating the name to leave without excuse any who should despise so evident a miracle, a thing seen, named, handled, and tasted by a whole nation (Calvin); the typology — the sweet white bread a figure of Christ, the true Manna, the Bread of Life (John 6:35, Ps 78:24).

themes: taste like wafers made with honey

schools.summary: The manna kept for a memorial — 'Fill an omer of it to be kept for your generations' (a single omer, one man's daily portion, laid up and preserved, not eaten); 'that they may see the bread wherewith I have fed you in the wilderness' (a witness to later generations who had not tasted it, of how God fed their fathers when there was no bread, the eye confirming the report); the omer laid up in a golden pot and kept in the ark, Heb 9:4 (Henry); set here by anticipation, the keeping belonging to a later time when the tabernacle was built, the more to confirm the manna a special creation of God's bounty (Calvin); the preserved manna not corrupting as the hoarded did, a standing miracle; God would have his mercies remembered.

themes: fill an omer to be kept
the bread wherewith I have fed you

schools.summary: The manna laid up before the LORD — 'take a pot, and put an omer full of manna therein, and lay it up before the LORD' (a vessel filled with the appointed omer and set apart); the pot — the Epistle to the Hebrews, following the LXX, calling it a 'golden pot' (Heb 9:4), while the Targum and Jarchi speak of an earthen one, from which Gill draws that an earthen vessel aptly emblems the ministers who bear the gospel treasure in earthen vessels; 'before the LORD' — before the divine presence and the Testimony, in or by the ark, among the sacred memorials (Ellicott); the manna given a place in the heart of the sanctuary beside the tables of the law; the keeping a priestly and holy charge given to Aaron.

themes: lay it up before the LORD

schools.summary: The obedient keeping of the manna — 'as the LORD commanded Moses, so Aaron laid it up before the Testimony' (the keeping done exactly as directed, the priest carrying out the command without alteration); 'before the Testimony' — before the ark of the testimony, so called because it held the tables of the law, the testimony of God's covenant (Gill); the Testimony and ark belonging to the tabernacle not yet built when the manna first fell, the actual laying-up accomplished later when the sanctuary was reared, the verse recording by anticipation (Clarke); the preservation of this manna, kept whole for generations when the daily manna would not last a night, itself a standing continual miracle, the fitter to be a memorial (Wesley); the kept manna testifying both to past feeding and to God's ongoing power.

themes: Aaron laid it up before the Testimony

schools.summary: The forty years of manna — 'the children of Israel did eat manna forty years, until they came to a land inhabited... unto the borders of the land of Canaan' (the heavenly bread not failing until they reached a settled fruitful land, forty years all but thirty days, from the Exodus to Gilgal, Gill); the constancy — a whole nation fed from heaven day by day for nearly four decades, the wonder in the long unbroken continuance; the question of authorship — the backward look leading some to suppose the book written after the manna ceased, answered that the verse may have been added by Moses near his death (the manna then some thirty-nine years and nine months old) or inserted later by Ezra under inspiration (Clarke, Ellicott); God's faithfulness feeding them all the way to the promised rest, the manna ceasing only when the land's bread began (Josh 5:12).

themes: until they came to a land inhabited

schools.summary: The explanatory note on the measure — 'an omer is the tenth part of an ephah' (the omer, each person's daily portion of manna, fixed in relation to the better-known ephah as a tenth of it, about six English pints, Clarke); the reason for the note — both omer and ephah originally Egyptian measures, but the ephah remaining in common use down to the captivity while the omer fell out of use very early, hence the parenthetic explanation added that later readers might understand the daily portion (Ellicott); the verse seeming out of its natural place, reading as if it belonged after the gathering (Ex 16:18) where the omer is first measured (Clarke); the care of Scripture to make the record plain to every generation.

themes: an omer is the tenth part of an ephah
